title-group.article-title: Insurance, race, and socioeconomic disparities in systemic therapy receipt for melanoma in the United States: a systematic review

contrib-group.aff: aDell Medical School, Austin, Texas, USA
bDepartment of Internal Medicine, Dell Medical School, Austin, Texas, USA
cDepartment of Medical Education, Dell Medical School, Austin, Texas, USA
dDivision of Dermatology, Dell Medical School, University of Texas, Austin, Texas, USA

fn.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/20450885.2026.2629903.

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Adewole S. Adamson adewole.adamson@austin.utexas.edu Department of Internal Medicine, Dell Medical School, Austin, TX, USA.

event.event-desc: published online in a building issue

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Although evidence suggests disparities in melanoma treatment, no comprehensive summary exists on whether insurance type, race/ethnicity, or socioeconomic status consistently influence systemic therapy receipt. Understanding these associations may inform strategies to reduce inequities.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We conducted a systematic review of United States (US) based studies assessing systemic therapy receipt by insurance, socioeconomic status, and race/ethnicity. Searches of PubMed, Embase, and Web of Science were performed through July 10, 2025. Evidence quality was evaluated using the Oxford Center for Evidence-Based Medicine.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Of 6,604 records screened and 223 full-texts reviewed, 30 retrospective cohort studies were included, encompassing 1,212,437 melanoma patients (1975–2021). Twenty-five studies stratified by insurance, 24 by socioeconomic status, and 21 by race/ethnicity. Insurance-based disparities were reported in 88% of studies, socioeconomic status-based disparities were reported in 75% of studies, and race/ethnicity- based disparities were reported in 38% of studies. Although 67% of studies used the National Cancer Database, findings were inconsistent across studies.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Insurance and socioeconomic status may be more consistent drivers of disparities in systemic therapy receipt for melanoma than race/ethnicity. However, variability in identifying disparities, even among studies using the same database, may complicate targeted interventions.

list-item.p: This systematic review analyzed 30 US-based retrospective cohort studies assessing the association between receipt of systemic therapy for melanoma and insurance type, socioeconomic status, and race/ethnicity.

Across 1.2 million melanoma patients between 1975 and 2021, delays or lack of receipt of systemic treatment were reported based on insurance type (88% of studies) and socioeconomic status (75%) and race and ethnicity (38%).
Two-thirds of included studies the same database, the National Cancer Database, yet findings of disparities were heterogeneous even among studies using this same data source.
Patterns suggest structural factors such as insurance coverage and socioeconomic resources may play a larger role than race/ethnicity in shaping access to systemic therapy for melanoma in the US.
Inconsistency in identifying disparities limits clear policy interventions and underscores the need for standardized approaches to measuring treatment equity in advanced melanoma care.
Our findings highlight opportunities for harmonizing analytic approaches in health outcomes and health disparities research.

funding-group.funding-statement: This manuscript was funded primarily by the American Cancer Society (Dr Adamson; Ms Cheek). Support was also received from the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation Harold Amos Faculty Development Program (Dr Adamson), the Dermatology Foundation Public Health Career Development Award (Dr Adamson), and a grant from the United States National Institutes of Health (No. UL1 TR002645; Dr Adamson). The funders had no role in study design, data collection and analysis, decision to publish, or preparation of the manuscript.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Among the most impactful developments in melanoma treatment is the advent of systemic therapies for metastatic disease, particularly, immunotherapy. These therapies significantly improved disease-specific and overall survival for metastatic melanoma [1]. The effect can even be seen at the population-level in the United States (US) manifested as a substantial decline in melanoma mortality since 2014, after 40 years of stable (or increasing) mortality [2].

As with many medical breakthroughs, benefits of innovations are often not realized by those with limited healthcare access [3,4]. This is the case for novel, expensive treatment, like targeted agents and immunotherapy, which cost many times the median salary of the average American [5]. Thus, the uninsured, and those of lower socioeconomic status (SES) may risk not receiving costly treatment. Furthermore, race and ethnicity have been associated with lack of receipt of cancer treatment in the US [6–8]. These sociodemographic factors encompass pillars of health disparities research [9]. They are among the most associated with disparate health outcomes and are commonly examined in population-based studies.

Understanding how key sociodemographic factors are associated with treatment receipt may inform policy and provisions of cancer care [10]. While studies detail association between these factors and systemic therapy receipt for melanoma, some have conflicting results. There is no comprehensive, systematic review assessing these key characteristics associated with health disparities (e.g., insurance, race, ethnicity, SES) and their relationship to systemic therapy receipt for melanoma. Herein, we aim to critically synthesize published data regarding this relationship. Because the sociopolitical context of health disparities differs across countries and their health systems, we focus on US-based studies.

sec.title: Personnel, protocol, and registration
sec.p: The systematic review protocol is registered in International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO) as ID CRD42023435596. We followed the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) updated reporting guideline [11]. Given the non-human participant nature, University of Texas at Austin IRB approval was not required.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: PubMed National Library of Medicine (NLM) followed by Embase (Elsevier) and Web of Science Core Collection (Clarivate) searches were conducted on August 7, 2023, and a final updated search on July 10, 2025. We acknowledge that the first Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved systemic therapy for melanoma was dacarbazine in 1975, but to be inclusive, there was no publication date restriction in case previous treatments were studied [1]. The research team and health sciences librarian (I.V.) designed the search strategy using keywords and Medical Subject Headings for two concepts: melanoma and health disparities. Melanoma search terms also included melanocytic freckle, malignant lentigo, and lentigo maligna. To find articles related to health disparities, we used the MEDLINE®/PubMed® Health Disparities and Minority Health Search Strategy, which was last updated in 2019 by the NLM [12]. The health sciences librarian added terms to update this search strategy and validated it against 12 ideal articles. She conducted the searches and deduplicated results in EndNote; additional duplicates were found by the Covidence screening tool and manually [13,14]. Full search strategy information is in Tables S1–3.

caption.p: Summary of study characteristics for systemic therapy, hospital- and facility-based studies.

tr.td: Receipt at the at the end of life
Receipt as the first systemic agent

fn.p: This table summarizes hospital- and facility-based retrospective cohort studies evaluating systemic therapy for melanoma. It includes details on study source, location, period, setting, melanoma stage, sample size, treatment type, measured outcomes, and disparities assessed by insurance type, race/ethnicity, and socioeconomic status (SES). Abbreviations: N/A = Not applicable; NCDB = National Cancer Database.

caption.p: Summary of study characteristics for systemic therapy, population-based studies.

tr.td: Stage I-III, Stage IV and above
In situ, localized, regional, distant metastasis, missing/ unknown

fn.p: This table presents population-based retrospective cohort studies examining systemic therapy for melanoma. It outlines study source, location, period, setting, melanoma stage, sample size, treatment type, measured outcomes, and disparities assessed by insurance type, race/ethnicity, and socioeconomic status (SES). Abbreviations: N/A = Not applicable; NCCCR = North Carolina Central Cancer Registry; SEER = Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results; TCR = Texas Cancer Registry.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: Articles were managed in Covidence, a standardized systematic review software for data management. Titles and abstracts were included after being screened by 2 reviewers (Shoshana Zhang (S.Z.), Rabecca-Kimberly Hernandez (R.K.H.), Jordan Cheek (J.C.), or Rija Aziz (R.A.)) based on defined criteria. Conflicts were settled by a third reviewer (Adewole S. Adamson (A.S.A.)). Articles lacking an abstract or sufficient information, but with potential for inclusion, were sent to full-text review. All full-texts meeting inclusion criteria were reviewed by 2 reviewers (S.Z., R.K.H., or R.A.) with conflicts resolved by a third reviewer (A.S.A.).

After database searches and screening, eligible articles were subject to forward and reverse searches conducted by 2 separate reviewers (S.Z. and R.K.H.). Reverse search involved reviewing all references listed in the final articles, while forward search utilized Google Scholar to identify records that cited the final articles. Records meeting inclusion criteria were then screened as appropriate. If there were discrepancies within study results, authors were contacted via email for clarification.

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
sec.p: Only articles from peer-reviewed journals and in English were included. Gray literature (i.e., conference abstracts, dissertations, popular press articles, letters, case reports, reviews, commentaries, editorials) were excluded. Studies lacking full-text were also excluded. Studies had to examine US populations.

Articles had to examine systemic therapy receipt for melanoma (i.e., chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy). A disparity was defined as a statistically significant lower likelihood of receipt of systemic therapy compared to reference group (e.g., White, private insurance, higher socioeconomic status). Studies that examined composite treatment (e.g., surgery, radiation, and immunotherapy) were excluded. Furthermore, articles had to analyze systemic therapy receipt by sociodemographic variables of insurance type, race and/or ethnicity, and/or socioeconomic status (SES). We focused on these variables as they have been most consistently studied and associated with cancer treatment disparities.

Insurance type could include any type of public or private insurance or being uninsured. Race and ethnicity included any variations of 5 categories outlined in the US Census Bureau: White or Caucasian, Black or African American, Asian or Pacific Islander, American Indian or Alaska Native, and Hispanic. Race could also include ‘non-White,’ which is often reported in studies given how rare melanoma is in people who do not identify as non-Hispanic White. We also included “Caucasian” directly from studies that used this terminology [15,16]. Though the term has problematic origins, historically, it has been used in medical research [17,18].

Socioeconomic status (SES) is a key factor in health research, as it influences healthcare access. SES can be measured using various indicators, which can be combined or analyzed separately. We included studies that examined SES measures individually (and composite) including but not limited to: income, education, geographic location (e.g., zip code or urban vs rural, border vs non-border) [19–21]. These included SES metrics were based on a definition provided by the NIH [21].

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: Two reviewers (S.Z. and R.K.H.) independently assessed the quality of included studies based on the Oxford Center for Evidence-Based Medicine Levels of Evidence [22]. Disagreements were resolved via discussion with a third reviewer (A.S.A.). Studies were graded into levels based on the “Therapy/Prevention, Aetiology/Harm” guideline section with level 1a being of highest quality (i.e., systematic reviews with homogeneity of randomized control trials) and level 5 being of lowest quality (i.e., opinion pieces).

sec.title: Data synthesis and statistical analysis
sec.p: Authors, publication year, study location and date, study type and setting, number of patients, treatment and disparity type, quality assessment, metric category and characteristics, and odds ratio or relative risk (including 95% Confidence Interval (CI) and P value) were extracted from each article. Authors were contacted by email for studies that were missing data or had discrepancies in results. If there was no response or if requested data was not available, information was marked “Not provided.”

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 9,770 records were identified (Figure 1). After removing 3,166 duplicates, 6,604 unique records underwent title and abstract screening. Of these, 6,381 were excluded, leaving 223 full-texts for review. We excluded 202 of these full-texts, since either the intervention/outcome was wrong (e.g., outcome was composite treatment), the study design was wrong (e.g., physician survey), or the patient population was wrong (e.g., non-small cell lung cancer). Twenty-three articles met inclusion criteria. Forward and reverse searches were conducted, uncovering 7 additional studies. The final 30 studies are presented in 2 tables based on study setting. Table 1 shows studies that used hospital- or facility-based data, while Table 2 presents studies that used population-based data.

caption.p: This figure illustrates the systematic review process for identifying studies on systemic therapy for melanoma. It shows the number of records imported from databases (Embase, PubMed, Web of Science), duplicates removed, studies screened, full-text articles assessed, and reasons for exclusion (wrong intervention/outcome, wrong study design, wrong patient population). The final number of studies included in the analysis is also indicated.

sec.title: Overview of study characteristics
sec.p: Thirty studies met inclusion criteria (Tables 1 and 2), and the years included were 1975–2021. There were a total of 1,212,437 patients. All studies were retrospective cohort studies in which the settings were hospital-based (67%) [3,15,16,23–35, 37–39,44,49], population-based (30%) [40–43,45–48], and facility-based (3%) [36]. The lone ‘facility-based’ study included specific oncology clinics in the Flatiron Health Network. Most studies included immunotherapy receipt (96%) as an outcome. Studies also considered chemotherapy (22%)[25,34, 37,40, 42,43] and targeted therapy (19%)[32,34, 37,40,43] as systemic therapy receipt. The most used data source was National Cancer Database (NCDB) (67%), followed by Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results (SEER) Program (17%). Patient-or neighborhood-level and geographic variables of insurance, race, ethnicity, and SES were not always all captured within each study.

sec.title: Disparities by insurance type
sec.p: Systemic therapy receipt by insurance type was investigated in 25 studies and its measurement varied across studies but usually included private and/or commercial, Medicare, other government, Military/Tricare/Veteran Affairs (VA), Medicaid, and uninsured (Table S4). Nearly every study including insurance-based disparities analysis, 88% (n = 22), showed insurance-based disparities in systemic therapy receipt for melanoma [3,15,16,23,24,26–28, 30–32,34,35,37–41, 46–49]. Furthermore, among 19 hospital-based studies that examined insurance-based disparities using NCDB, 17 (85%) found insurance-based disparities [3,15,16,23,24,26–28, 30–32,34,35,37–39,49]. Meanwhile, all 5 population-based studies analyzing insurance-based disparities found insurance-based disparities [40,41,46–48]. Many studies showed that uninsured patients and those with Medicaid were less likely to receive systemic therapy for melanoma compared to privately-insured patients [3,23, 26,27,30–32,34,35,37, 40,41,46,48].

In comparison, only 3 studies (12%) including insurance-based disparities analysis showed no treatment disparity based on insurance type, and all 3 used NCDB [25,29,33]. These were Frey et al, which investigated immunotherapy usage for stage IIIA melanoma between 2015 and 2019, Kerekes et al, which examined end of life immunotherapy initiation between 2012 and 2019, and Dhaliwal 2023, which studied immunotherapy or chemotherapy receipt for stage III and IV melanoma between 2011 and 2018. All 3 studies used the same insurance categories: none (i.e., uninsured), private, Medicare, Medicaid, and other government.

sec.title: Disparities by race and ethnicity
sec.p: Systemic therapy receipt by race and/or ethnicity was explored in 21 studies [3,15,16,23–30, 32–34,36, 39,44–47,49], among those 38% (n = 8) found a disparity [15,16,23,24, 27,30, 32,49]. Race and ethnicity varied in how it was measured including, but not limited to: White, Caucasian, Black, Hispanic, Asian/Pacific Islander, other, unknown (Table S4). The 4 population-based studies that examined race-based disparities all found no race-based disparities in systemic therapy receipt [44–47]. Although most studies did not find race- and ethnicity-based disparities in systemic therapy receipt [3,25,26,28,29,33,34,36, 39,45–47], there were 8 studies that did show disparity all used NCDB for analysis and focused on systemic therapy for advanced stage disease [15,16,23,24, 27,30, 32,49].

Despite all these studies also using the same database, racial disparities were not consistently associated with specific racial groups. In Sussman et al, investigators showed that African American (OR 5.88, 95%CI: 1.60–28.58) patients were more likely to receive immunotherapy than Caucasian (OR 0.536, 95% CI: 0.301–0.942), Asian (0.266, 95% CI: 0.073–0.875), and race-unspecified (0.170, 95% CI: 0.035–0.624) patients from 2015 to 2016 [16]. However, there was no difference in immunotherapy receipt between Hispanic and African American patients (OR 0.612, 95% CI: 0.309–1.199). In contrast, Kakish et al found that compared to non-Hispanic White patients, non-Hispanic Black patients (OR 0.69, 95% CI: 0.48–0.98) were less likely to receive systemic therapy for melanoma later in 2016 to 2020 [32]. Asian (OR 1.17, 95% CI: 0.69–1.97) and Hispanic (OR 1.10, 95% CI 0.87–1.40) patients did not differ in the likelihood of systemic therapy receipt compared to non-Hispanic White patients during the same years. However, patients that identified as other/unknown (OR 1.36, 35% CI: 1.05–1.77) were more likely to receive treatment. Similar to Kakish et al, in Haque et al, African American (OR 0.626, 95% CI: 0.408–0.959) patients had lower odds of receiving immunotherapy than White patients [30].

In contrast, in Ermer et al, investigators studied immunotherapy receipt pre- (2007–2010) and post-approval (2011–2013) and showed lower risk of immunotherapy receipt only among Hispanic (OR 0.28, 95% CI 0.10–0.76) vs non-Hispanic White patients in the pre-approval era [27]. Other groups including Black and other patients showed no difference in receipt in either era. In comparison, in Mulligan 2024, there was no difference in adjuvant therapy receipt between non-Hispanic White (reference), Black (OR 1.06, 95% CI 0.68–1.64), and Asian (OR 1.16, 95% CI 0.67–2.01) patients [49]. However, Hispanic (OR 1.34, 95% CI 1.02–1.74) vs non-Hispanic White patients were more likely to receive adjuvant therapy whereas others/unknown (OR 0.61, 95% CI 0.45–0.83) vs non-Hispanic White patients were less likely to receive adjuvant therapy. Similarly, Coogan et al showed that Hispanic patients (OR 1.523, 95% CI 1.117–2.077) had higher odds of immunotherapy receipt compared to non-Hispanic patients [24]. Finally, Conic et al reported that patients of unknown race (OR 0.46, 95%CI 0.23–0.82) were less likely to receive immunotherapy compared to Caucasian patients [15].

sec.title: Disparities by socioeconomic status
sec.p: Systemic therapy receipt by SES was considered in 24 studies, among which 75% (n = 18) showed a disparity [3,16, 23,25, 27,28,30,32, 34,35,37,39, 42–46,49]. SES measurement varied substantially across studies and included geography indicators (e.g., rural vs urban), income proxies (e.g., household income), educational measures (e.g., education attainment or graduations rate), or composite indices. Supplemental Table S4 details the SES categories used in each study, including the specific granular groupings, which collectively reflect a wide range of SES indicators. SES disparities that were demonstrated within studies included lower likelihood of systemic therapy receipt by high-school education/graduation, income, social vulnerability, and SES score combining education and income, and poverty index [3,16, 23,27,28,30, 32,34,35,37, 39,42, 44–46]. These SES factors were examined at the zip code- or county-level (Table S4). The hospital-based studies examining SES-based disparities (n = 17) more consistently found SES-based disparities than the population-based studies examining SES-based disparities (n = 7). Though a majority of studies showed a disparity in systemic therapy receipt by SES, 6 (25%) did not show a disparity [24,29, 33,38, 40,48].

In Adamson et al, which used a population-based database in North Carolina, rural zip code at diagnosis was not associated with a lower likelihood of systemic therapy receipt compared to urban zip code [40]. In another NCDB-based study of patients with anorectal melanoma, Taylor et al found that patient geographic residence (urban/rural vs metro vs unknown) was not associated with differences in likelihood of immunotherapy receipt [38]. For Frey et al, utilization of immunotherapy among stage IIIA melanoma patients did not differ by geography (urban vs rural zip code) or median income among patients in NCDB [29]. Kerekes et al, also used NCDB and found no difference by zip code-based median income in end-of-life immunotherapy receipt in patients with metastatic melanoma [33]. In a study using the Texas Cancer Registry, Ramirez et al found no difference in immunotherapy receipt in patients from border vs non-border regions [48]. Lastly, Coogan et al demonstrated that immunotherapy receipt was not significantly impacted by income or education quartiles [24].

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: All studies were graded 2b given their retrospective cohort study design, deeming them of moderate quality based on the Center for Evidence-Based Medicine at Oxford guidelines [22].

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: We examined disparities in systemic therapy receipt for melanoma in the US, focusing on the key sociodemographic factors of insurance, race and ethnicity, and SES. Among these 3 measures, we found that insurance type was most consistently related to disparities in systemic therapy receipt, followed by SES. Race and ethnicity were less commonly associated with disparities in therapy receipt. All studies met the 2b-level of evidence, which reflects retrospective cohort studies of moderate quality. However, we found that the heterogeneity of study design and specific measures used for insurance, race and ethnicity, and SES within the studies precluded meta-analysis.

Among insurance type analysis, we found that being insured (e.g., private insurance or Medicare) was overwhelmingly associated with increased likelihood of systemic therapy receipt when compared to being uninsured, and in some cases, having Medicaid. This finding is likely due to the high cost of systemic therapy, particularly immunotherapy. Most patients can only access this potentially lifesaving treatment with insurance. Nevertheless, 3 studies, Frey et al, Kerekes et al, and Dhaliwal et al, did not find any insurance-based disparities, despite using NCDB as a resource [25,29,33]. Most other studies using NCDB found insurance-based disparities related to systemic therapy receipt. This divergence from other data may be due to analytic choice or analyzing restrictive patient populations: Frey et al focused on stage IIIA melanoma and Kerekes et al studied end of life utilization [29,33].

In most studies, patient race and ethnicity were not associated with receipt of systemic therapy. However, this finding should be interpreted with caution. Melanoma predominantly affects individuals of non-Hispanic White race, resulting in substantially smaller sample sizes for other racial and ethnic groups. In some studies, these numbers may be insufficient to support meaningful comparisons, limiting statistical power and potentially obscuring true disparities. Nevertheless, when disparity was discovered, the findings were inconsistent across racial groups. For example, Sussman et al and Kakish et al both used NCDB, yet arrived at opposite results: Sussman et al found that African American patients were more likely to receive immunotherapy than “Caucasian” patients (a problematic term used by the authors to refer to non-Hispanic White patients) whereas Kakish et al found that Black patients were less likely to receive immunotherapy than White patients [16,32]. Ermer et al found that Hispanic patients received less immunotherapy than White patients, while Mulligan et al and Coogan et al found that Hispanic patients were more likely to receive immunotherapy than White/non-Hispanic patients [24,27,49]. These studies all used NCDB, so it is remarkable how the same database yielded opposing, inconsistent associations. However, since melanoma affects mostly non-Hispanic White individuals (>95%), race and ethnicity disparities may be difficult to assess given lower numbers [50]. This may explain why 10 studies did not include race and ethnicity in their analysis [31,35, 37,38,40–42,48]. Notably, 6 of the 8 studies that found a disparity by race and ethnicity also demonstrated disparities by the 2 other measures of interest: insurance type and SES [16,23, 27,30, 32,49]. Nevertheless, given that race and ethnicity may be powerful risk factors associated with receipt of guideline-based cancer care, it is reassuring that few studies identified disparities in melanoma therapy across these important sociopolitical measures [51,52]. However, further research is needed to more rigorously evaluate this association.

In SES analysis, we found that disparity in systemic therapy receipt by SES was inconsistently measured with 7 studies not evaluating SES [15,26, 31,36, 41,47]. We considered both contextual- and individual-level SES together in our analysis, and the supplement details how the measures were specifically coded. However, Studies used a wide variety of categories and characteristics classified under SES: rural vs urban geography, household income, education attainment, and composite variables. We hypothesized that studies showing insurance-based disparity would also demonstrate SES-based disparities, however this was not always the case. Much of the inconsistencies across studies, including those using the same database (i.e., NCDB), may be due the lacking standardized measures of SES. This is problematic because the heterogeneity makes designing policy interventions more difficult. Fortunately, there have been efforts to standardize how SES is measured, since SES is important for patient outcomes across various cancer types [20,21].

What stands out most is that study design likely played a significant role in whether or not disparities were found. This is especially apparent considering that a majority of studies (67%) used a single database (NCDB) for analysis. Despite using a common data source, investigators reported results ranging from no disparities across categories to disparities in all categories. This variation likely reflects analytic choices made by investigators for their studies, a phenomenon that has been termed ‘vibration of effects.’[53,54] Analytic flexibility makes it difficult to consistently identify disparities so that interventions can be developed, and their effects measured.

Given the heterogeneity of findings related to healthcare disparities, our systematic review suggests that a core set of disparity measures should be adopted, or at minimum, a standard language around disparities should be developed. However, such standard should allow for nuance, as some disparities may be context dependent.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: No standard definition of reporting health disparities exists. However, we attempted to minimize this challenge by using an expansive and NIH-developed strategy [12]. We focused exclusively on lack of receipt of systemic treatment; however, this is only one perspective in which to look at treatment disparities. Future studies could examine disparities in time to receipt of treatment, type of treatment received, or survival after diagnosis. Despite our exhaustive review, some articles may have been missed, although we attempted to limit this through forward and reverse searches. We did not explore every aspect of SES or other patient, tumor, or health system factors, which may also contribute to healthcare disparities. Because many of these studies are not population-based, we are limited in getting the most comprehensive understanding of how findings vary across time and place. At least 4 out of 30 studies potentially included use of adjuvant immunotherapy in which various unaccounted factors could have influenced receipt of systemic therapy. Finally, given the restriction to studies conducted on US populations, our findings are not applicable to healthcare disparities in other countries. All included studies were also only of moderate quality.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: In this systematic review of over 1 million patients with melanoma across 45 years in the US, we found that insurance type and SES were most consistently associated with systemic therapy receipt. Race and ethnicity were less frequent drivers of disparity in systemic therapy receipt. However, high variability in identifying disparities across studies using the same database (NCDB) is concerning, as it potentially complicates the design of effective interventions and allocation of resources. Further high-quality work in understanding source(s) of disparity (if any) for melanoma treatment using systemic therapies is needed.

sec.title: Ethical disclosure
sec.p: The University of Texas at Austin Institutional Review Board (IRB) deemed this study exempt.

sec.title: Author contributions
sec.p: All authors contributed to the conceptualization and methodology of the study. Ms. Zhang and Ms. Hernandez were responsible for data curation, validation, and visualization, and together with Dr. Adamson performed formal analysis. Dr. Adamson obtained funding, provided resources, and oversaw project administration and supervision. All authors participated in investigation and contributed to writing: Ms. Zhang, Ms. Hernandez, and Dr. Adamson prepared the original draft, and all authors were involved in review and editing of the manuscript.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: The authors have no relevant affiliations or financial involvement with any organization or entity with a financial interest in or financial conflict with the subject matter or materials discussed in the manuscript. This includes employment, consultancies, honoraria, stock ownership or options, expert testimony, grants or patents received or pending, or royalties.
No writing assistance was utilized in the production of this manuscript.

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: The data underlying this article are available in the article and online supplementary material.

ref-list.title: References
ref-list.p: Papers of special note have been highlighted as either of interest (•) or of considerable interest (••) to readers.

ref.mixed-citation: *Curti BD, Faries MB. Recent advances in the treatment of melanoma. N Engl J Med. 2021;384(23):2229–2240. doi: 10.1056/NEJMra203486134107182

note.p: Explanation: This manuscript is of interest because it succinctly reviews the major advances that have transformed melanoma treatment, particularly immunotherapy.

ref.mixed-citation: Welch HG, Mazer BL, Adamson AS. The rapid rise in cutaneous melanoma diagnoses. N Engl J Med. 2021;384(1):72–79. doi: 10.1056/NEJMsb201976033406334

Al-Qurayshi Z, Crowther JE, Hamner JB, et al. Disparities of immunotherapy utilization in patients with stage III cutaneous melanoma: A national perspective. Anticancer Res. 2018;38(5):2897–2901.29715114 10.21873/anticanres.12536

Osarogiagbon RU, Sineshaw HM, Unger JM, et al. Immune-based cancer treatment: Addressing disparities in access and outcomes. Am Soc Clin Oncol Educ Book. 2021;41(41):1–13. doi: 10.1200/EDBK_32352333830825

Verma V, Sprave T, Haque W, et al. A systematic review of the cost and cost-effectiveness studies of immune checkpoint inhibitors. J Immunother Cancer. 2018;6(1):128. doi: 10.1186/s40425-018-0442-730470252 PMC6251215

Freeman JQ, Li JL, Fisher SG, et al. Declination of treatment, racial and ethnic disparity, and overall survival in US patients with breast cancer. JAMA Netw Open. 2024;7(5):e249449. doi: 10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2024.944938722630 PMC11082691

Fwelo P, Yusuf ZI, Adjei A, et al. Racial and ethnic disparities in the refusal of surgical treatment in women 40 years and older with breast cancer in the USA between 2010 and 2017. Breast Cancer Res Treat. 2022;194(3):643–661. doi: 10.1007/s10549-022-06653-w35749020 PMC9287205
Shavers VL, Brown ML. Racial and ethnic disparities in the receipt of cancer treatment. J Natl Cancer Inst. 2002;94(5):334–357. doi: 10.1093/jnci/94.5.33411880473

*Pérez-Stable EJ, Webb Hooper M. The pillars of health disparities science—race, ethnicity, and socioeconomic status. JAMA Health Forum. 2023;4(12):e234463. doi: 10.1001/jamahealthforum.2023.446338127587

note.p: Explanation: This paper is of interest because it outlines the foundational roles of race, ethnicity, and socioeconomic status as core “pillars” shaping health disparities science and frames how research and policy can address inequities.

note.p: Explanation: This paper is of considerable interest because it shows how observational study conclusions can change substantially depending on analytic choices.
